description: HUD state management pattern using hierarchical file organization: local project state (.omc/state/hud-state.json), legacy local state (.omc/hud-state.json), and settings config (~/.claude/settings.json). Uses atomic writes to prevent corruption and merges configs with preset overrides.
code_snippet: const localStateFile = join(getOmcRoot(baseDir), 'state'); const settingsFile = join(getClaudeConfigDir(), 'settings.json'); const config = mergeWithDefaults({...legacyConfig, ...settings.omcHud});
relevance: Apply multi-level state organization to separate project-specific state, global config, and legacy paths. Use atomic writes to prevent corruption during concurrent access.

description: HUD rendering uses function composition where each element returns formatted strings or null. Elements are conditionally included based on config, then joined with dim separators. Includes ANSI code preservation during width measurement.
code_snippet: const headerLine = elements.length > 0 ? elements.join(dim(PLAIN_SEPARATOR)) : null; const ansiMatch = remaining.match(ANSI_REGEX); if (ansiMatch && ansiMatch.index === 0) { result += ansiMatch[0]; }
relevance: Use discrete, composable render functions with null-coalescing to skip disabled elements. Preserve ANSI codes during width measurement to avoid corrupting colors.

description: Context injector merges multiple sources using priority ordering (critical > high > normal > low) then timestamp. Supports three injection strategies: prepend, append, wrap in XML tags. Deduplicates entries by source:id key.
code_snippet: const PRIORITY_ORDER = { critical: 0, high: 1, normal: 2, low: 3 }; entries.sort((a,b) => PRIORITY_ORDER[a.priority] - PRIORITY_ORDER[b.priority] || a.timestamp - b.timestamp); const merged = entries.map(e => e.content).join(CONTEXT_SEPARATOR);
relevance: Use priority + timestamp sorting to deterministically order context. Deduplication by source:id prevents duplicates. Wrap strategy enables clear separation.

description: Payload validation checks: (1) top-level key count to prevent key explosion, (2) nesting depth to prevent stack overflow, (3) serialized byte size to prevent OOM. Returns detailed error messages with actual/max values.
code_snippet: const keyCount = Object.keys(payload).length; if (keyCount > resolved.maxTopLevelKeys) return {valid: false}; const depth = measureDepth(payload); const byteSize = Buffer.byteLength(JSON.stringify(payload));
relevance: Apply three-tier validation (key count, depth, size) to prevent memory exhaustion. Use Buffer.byteLength for accurate UTF-8 sizing.

description: Dual debouncing: RAPID_FIRE_DEBOUNCE_MS (500ms) skips analysis when concurrent tool outputs arrive; COMPACTION_COOLDOWN_MS rate-limits warnings. Per-session state tracks cumulative tokens, warning count, last-warning timestamp. Resets on session stop.
code_snippet: const RAPID_FIRE_DEBOUNCE_MS = 500; const now = Date.now(); if (now - lastAnalysis < RAPID_FIRE_DEBOUNCE_MS) return null; state.estimatedTokens += responseTokens; if (now - state.lastWarningTime < cooldownMs) return false;
relevance: Use rapid-fire debounce to handle concurrent async operations. Per-session tracking enables stateful monitoring. Reset on natural boundaries.

description: Prevents unbounded process accumulation by tracking PID and timestamp. Checks if previous PID is alive using process.kill(pid, 0). Combines PID check with 120s throttle to guard against accumulation.
code_snippet: if (summaryProcessPid !== null) { try { process.kill(summaryProcessPid, 0); return; } catch { summaryProcessPid = null; } } if (now - lastSummarySpawnTimestamp < 120_000) return; child.unref();
relevance: Use process.kill(pid, 0) to check liveness before respawning. Combine with timestamp throttle. Use child.unref() for fire-and-forget tasks.

description: Config resolution: defaults < preset overrides < user settings < legacy compatibility. Presets (minimal, focused, full, dense, opencode) provide sensible defaults. User settings override presets. Merges preserve legacy values.
code_snippet: const preset = config.preset ?? DEFAULT_HUD_CONFIG.preset; const presetElements = PRESET_CONFIGS[preset]; return { ...DEFAULT_HUD_CONFIG.elements, ...presetElements, ...config.elements };
relevance: Use preset configs to reduce configuration burden. Layer overrides cleanly. Preserve legacy values for backward compatibility.

description: ContextCollector manages per-session context via Map<sessionId, Map<sourceId, ContextEntry>>. Deduplication key is source:id. getPending returns merged context non-destructively; consume clears after return. Supports entry removal.
code_snippet: private sessions: Map<string, Map<string, ContextEntry>> = new Map(); const key = `${options.source}:${options.id}`; const pending = this.getPending(sessionId); this.clear(sessionId); return pending;
relevance: Use nested maps for session-scoped state. Separate getPending (non-destructive) from consume (destructive). Support session enumeration.

description: Truncation preserves unicode by measuring code points (handles surrogate pairs), skipping ANSI during width calculation, appending ANSI reset before ellipsis to prevent bleed. Uses getCharWidth() for visual width.
code_snippet: const codePoint = line.codePointAt(i); const codeUnits = codePoint > 0xffff ? 2 : 1; const charWidth = getCharWidth(char); if (visibleWidth + charWidth > targetWidth) break; const reset = hasAnsi ? '\u001b[0m' : '';
relevance: Handle unicode properly by measuring code points. Preserve ANSI codes but measure visible width only. Reset ANSI before ellipsis.

description: Prevents jitter by caching previous stdin values and applying stabilization. readStdinCache persists last value. stabilizeContextPercent smooths rapid swings. Stores display scope (session ID or cwd) for consistency.
code_snippet: const previousStdinCache = readStdinCache(); stdin = stabilizeContextPercent(stdin, previousStdinCache); contextDisplayScope: currentSessionId ?? cwd
relevance: Cache and smooth rapidly-fluctuating metrics. Normalize display scope to session ID or cwd. Persist cache for --watch fallback.

description: Atomic JSON and file writes using temp file with UUID suffix + fsync + atomic rename. Ensures durability even on crash between write and rename. Also implements directory fsync for best-effort rename durability.
code_snippet: const tempPath = path.join(dir, `.${base}.tmp.${crypto.randomUUID()}`); const fd = await fs.open(tempPath, 'wx', 0o600); await fd.write(jsonContent, 0, 'utf-8'); await fd.sync(); await fd.close(); await fs.rename(tempPath, filePath); // directory fsync for durability
relevance: Researchers repo needs atomic writes for concurrent state updates in multi-agent dispatch. Pattern prevents data corruption when multiple agents write state simultaneously.

description: Cross-process advisory file locking using O_CREAT|O_EXCL flag. Kernel guarantees only one process succeeds. Includes PID-based stale lock detection (checks process alive), automatic reaping of dead locks, and optional retry/timeout with deadline-based polling.
code_snippet: const fd = openSync(lockPath, fsConstants.O_CREAT | fsConstants.O_EXCL | fsConstants.O_WRONLY, 0o600); const payload = JSON.stringify({pid: process.pid, timestamp: Date.now()}); if (isLockStale(lockPath, staleLockMs)) { unlinkSync(lockPath); } // retry after reaping
relevance: Multi-agent dispatch needs exclusive access to shared resources. File locking prevents race conditions in concurrent task execution and state mutations.

description: Shared memory layer using filesystem-based KV store at .omc/state/shared-memory/{namespace}/{key}.json. Namespaces provide isolation. Each entry includes metadata (createdAt, updatedAt, ttl, expiresAt). TTL support with automatic expiry cleanup. Path validation prevents directory traversal.
code_snippet: const SHARED_MEMORY_DIR = 'state/shared-memory'; function validateNamespace(ns: string) { if (!/^[a-zA-Z0-9][a-zA-Z0-9._-]*$/.test(ns)) throw new Error(...); } export function writeEntry(namespace: string, key: string, value: unknown, ttl?: number) { const entry = {key, value, namespace, createdAt, updatedAt, ttl, expiresAt: ttl ? new Date(Date.now() + ttl*1000).toISOString() : undefined}; }
relevance: Researchers multi-agent coordination requires cross-session memory. Namespace isolation supports /team and /pipeline workflows. TTL enables auto-cleanup of stale inter-agent messages.

description: Canonical session isolation check function to prevent cross-session state leakage. Three modes: (1) no session - allow all, (2) legacy ownerless state - reject by default unless lenient, (3) session mismatch - always reject. Consistent semantics across all state-checking code paths.
code_snippet: export function isStateForSession(stateSessionId: string | undefined | null, sessionId: string | undefined | null, options?: {lenient?: boolean}): boolean { if (!sessionId) return true; if (!stateSessionId) return options?.lenient === true; return stateSessionId === sessionId; }
relevance: Prevents multi-agent contamination. Each agent/session verifies ownership before reading/modifying shared state. Critical for isolation guarantees in concurrent dispatch.

description: Cross-tool task/message passing using atomicWriteJsonSync + file locks. Task status tracking (pending/in_progress/completed/failed) with completion timestamps. Zod schemas for validation. Lock-protected read-modify-write on updateSharedTask ensures concurrent updates don't lose changes.
code_snippet: export function updateSharedTask(cwd, taskId, updates) { const taskPath = join(getInteropDir(cwd), 'tasks', `${taskId}.json`); return withFileLockSync(taskPath + '.lock', () => { const parsed = SharedTaskSchema.safeParse(JSON.parse(readFileSync(taskPath, 'utf-8'))); const updatedTask = {...task, ...updates}; if (updates.status === 'completed' && !updatedTask.completedAt) { updatedTask.completedAt = new Date().toISOString(); } atomicWriteJsonSync(taskPath, updatedTask); }); }
relevance: Researchers dispatch needs reliable task coordination between agents. Lock-protected updates prevent concurrent modifications from stomping each other. Status tracking enables robust async workflows.

description: Two-level cache for state reads to avoid repeated disk I/O. Cache hit requires: (1) mtime matches pre-read stat, (2) cache TTL (5s) not expired, (3) mtime unchanged post-read. Prevents TOCTOU race where concurrent write between stat and read corrupts cache. Uses structuredClone for deep copies.
code_snippet: const statBefore = fs.statSync(path); const mtimeBefore = statBefore.mtimeMs; const cached = stateCache.get(path); if (cached && cached.mtime === mtimeBefore && Date.now() - cached.cachedAt < STATE_CACHE_TTL_MS) return cached; const data = JSON.parse(fs.readFileSync(path, 'utf-8')); const statAfter = fs.statSync(path); if (statAfter.mtimeMs === mtimeBefore) { stateCache.set(path, {data: structuredClone(data), mtime: mtimeBefore}); }
relevance: Reduces load on state I/O during high-concurrency multi-agent scenarios. TOCTOU guards prevent serving stale cached state. structuredClone isolation prevents cache pollution.

description: Wraps read-modify-write in exclusive file lock using O_CREAT|O_EXCL. Invalidates cache under lock before reading to force fresh disk read. Prevents stale cached data from being base for concurrent updates. Pattern: acquire lock, invalidate cache, read current, compute new, write, release.
code_snippet: export class StateManager<T> { update(updater: (current: T | undefined) => T): boolean { const statePath = getStatePath(this.name, this.location); return withFileLock(statePath, () => { stateCache.delete(statePath); const current = this.get(); const updated = updater(current); return this.set(updated); }); } }
relevance: Multi-agent state updates need atomicity. Lock + cache invalidation ensures each agent sees consistent state during its transaction. Prevents lost updates in concurrent scenarios.

description: Loads skills from bundled SKILL.md files in skills/{name}/SKILL.md. Parses YAML frontmatter for metadata (name, description, model, agent, aliases). Deduplicates via case-insensitive lookup. Avoids shadowing native commands (review, plan, init, etc.) by prefixing with 'omc-'. Caches loaded skills.
code_snippet: const CC_NATIVE_COMMANDS = new Set(['review', 'plan', 'init', ...]); function toSafeSkillName(name) { return CC_NATIVE_COMMANDS.has(name.toLowerCase()) ? `omc-${name}` : name; } function loadSkillFromFile(skillPath) { const {metadata, body} = parseFrontmatter(content); const safeName = toSafeSkillName(metadata.name); return [{name: safeName, template: body, ...metadata}]; } let cachedSkills = null; export function createBuiltinSkills() { if (!cachedSkills) cachedSkills = loadSkillsFromDirectory(); return cachedSkills; }
relevance: Researchers needs extensible skills system for custom agent prompts. Frontmatter metadata enables skill configuration. Caching supports efficient runtime lookup in high-throughput dispatch.

description: Detects stale state by checking both updatedAt and heartbeatAt timestamps. State is NOT stale if EITHER timestamp is recent (within maxAgeMs, default 4 hours). Allows long-running workflows with periodic heartbeats to survive stale checks. Separate cleanup function marks stale states inactive without side-effects in reader.
code_snippet: export function isStateStale(meta: {updatedAt?: string; heartbeatAt?: string}, now: number, maxAgeMs: number) { const updatedAt = meta.updatedAt ? new Date(meta.updatedAt).getTime() : undefined; const heartbeatAt = meta.heartbeatAt ? new Date(meta.heartbeatAt).getTime() : undefined; if (updatedAt && now - updatedAt <= maxAgeMs) return false; if (heartbeatAt && now - heartbeatAt <= maxAgeMs) return false; return true; }
relevance: Supports long-running multi-step workflows in dispatch. Heartbeat mechanism keeps state alive without full updates. Separate cleanup prevents reader side-effects. Handles extended agent chains.

description: Scores workers for task assignment using three additive components: capability fitness score (0-1), a load penalty (-0.2 per assigned task), an idle bonus (+0.1), and a +0.3 bonus when the task description language matches the worker role via lane-intent inference. Final score is clamped to [0,1]. Workers with zero fitness are excluded entirely.
code_snippet: const fitnessScore = scoreWorkerFitness(worker, caps); const loadPenalty = currentLoad * 0.2; const idleBonus = worker.status === "idle" ? 0.1 : 0; const intentBonus = laneIntent !== "unknown" && workerMatchesIntent(worker, laneIntent) ? 0.3 : 0; const finalScore = Math.min(1, Math.max(0, fitnessScore - loadPenalty + idleBonus + intentBonus));
relevance: claude-multi-agent-dispatch task allocation can use this composite scoring model to route crawl subtasks to the most suitable spider/agent type while automatically balancing load

description: Each worker atomically writes a heartbeat JSON file every poll cycle. The lead checks freshness with isWorkerAlive(maxAgeMs): if the lastPollAt timestamp is older than maxAgeMs, or the file is missing, or the date is invalid, the worker is declared dead. listHeartbeats scans all *.heartbeat.json files to get a fleet-wide health snapshot.
code_snippet: export function isWorkerAlive(workingDirectory, teamName, workerName, maxAgeMs) { const heartbeat = readHeartbeat(...); if (!heartbeat) return false; const lastPoll = new Date(heartbeat.lastPollAt).getTime(); if (isNaN(lastPoll)) return false; return (Date.now() - lastPoll) < maxAgeMs; }
relevance: The researchers repo Scrapy spiders and dispatch workers have no liveness checks; adding heartbeat files would let the agenttasks dashboard detect hung workers and trigger restarts

description: Uses git merge-tree --write-tree (Git 2.38+) for a dry-run three-way merge simulation to detect conflicts before touching the working tree. Falls back to a file-overlap heuristic (comparing diff --name-only from merge-base) for older Git versions. Actual merges always use --no-ff and abort on failure to keep the repo clean.
relevance: When multiple claude-multi-agent workers each edit documentation or code files in parallel worktrees, this pre-check prevents wasted merge attempts and surfaces conflicts early for human resolution

description: Uses a monotonic next_worker_index counter in the team config to guarantee unique worker names across the full team lifetime (not just current roster). Scale-up is wrapped in a file-based scaling lock. If any pane spawn fails, the rollback function removes already-added workers from config and kills their tmux panes. scale_down uses a 3-phase protocol: set draining status, wait for idle/done (up to drainTimeoutMs), then kill panes.
code_snippet: let nextIndex = config.next_worker_index ?? (currentCount + 1); const workerName = ; // ... spawn pane ...; config.next_worker_index = nextIndex; await saveTeamConfig(config, leaderCwd); // rollback: config.workers.splice(idx, 1); config.next_worker_index = initialNextIndex;
relevance: The researchers dispatch system can apply this monotonic-index pattern to uniquely identify retried or dynamically-added agents without name collisions, and use the 3-phase drain before worker teardown

description: A quality/safety gate (checkSentinelReadiness) that runs factcheck + sentinel health checks before dispatch. Fail-closed: if the gate is enabled but no checks are provided (no logPath, no claims), it blocks rather than passes. Blockers are deduplicated. waitForSentinelReadiness adds a polling loop with configurable timeout and poll interval, returning attempts count and elapsedMs for observability.
code_snippet: if (!ranCheck) return { ready: false, blockers: ["gate enabled but no logPath or claims — cannot verify readiness"], skipped: true }; const dedupedBlockers = [...new Set(blockers)]; return { ready: dedupedBlockers.length === 0, blockers: dedupedBlockers, skipped: false };
relevance: Before dispatching scraped data or agent results in the researchers pipeline, a sentinel gate can validate file claims and log health to catch hallucinated outputs or corrupt crawl artifacts before they propagate

description: Replaces a polling done.json watchdog loop with event-driven lifecycle transitions (claim-task, transition-task-status). A feature flag (OMC_RUNTIME_V2 env var) allows opt-out to legacy v1 behavior. Completion is detected via API lifecycle events and worker heartbeat/status files rather than file polling, eliminating sleep-based waits. Preserves sentinel gate and circuit breaker from v1.
code_snippet: export function isRuntimeV2Enabled(env = process.env) { const raw = env.OMC_RUNTIME_V2; if (!raw) return true; return !["0", "false", "no", "off"].includes(raw.trim().toLowerCase()); } // NO done.json polling — completion via CLI API lifecycle transitions
relevance: The researchers repo multi-agent runtime still relies on polling loops; adopting this event-driven pattern with a feature flag for rollback would reduce latency and CPU overhead in the dispatch and crawl pipelines

description: Uses O_EXCL mkdir as an atomic lock primitive for mutual exclusion on the dispatch queue. Writes an owner token (pid.timestamp.random) to verify ownership on release. Detects stale locks by checking mtime against LOCK_STALE_MS. Implements exponential backoff with jitter on contention, bounded by configurable timeout.
code_snippet:
await mkdir(lockDir, { recursive: false }); // O_EXCL throws EEXIST if locked
const ownerToken = String(process.pid) + "." + Date.now(); // verify ownership on release
if (Date.now() - info.mtimeMs > LOCK_STALE_MS) { await rm(lockDir, { recursive: true, force: true }); continue; }
pollMs = Math.min(pollMs * 2, DISPATCH_LOCK_MAX_POLL_MS); // exponential backoff + jitter
relevance: claude-multi-agent-dispatch can use this pattern for safe concurrent writes to shared dispatch/queue JSON files without requiring a database or external lock service

description: Deduplicates pending dispatch requests before enqueuing. For mailbox kind uses message_id as the dedup key; for inbox kind uses inbox_correlation_key; for nudge kind falls back to trigger_message equality. Only deduplicates against currently pending requests (not delivered/failed ones).
relevance: Prevents duplicate task dispatches in claude-multi-agent-dispatch when a crawl job or agent task is submitted multiple times due to retries or concurrent triggers

description: Enforces a strict state machine for dispatch request lifecycle: pending -> notified -> delivered, or pending/notified -> failed. canTransitionDispatchStatus is the single source of truth for allowed transitions, executed inside the lock with atomic file writes. Timestamps (notified_at, delivered_at, failed_at) are stamped automatically on each transition.
relevance: Task status management in agenttasks and claude-multi-agent-sdk can use this gated transition pattern to prevent illegal state jumps such as marking a task complete before it was claimed

description: Each worker inbox is a JSONL append-only file. Reads use a persistent byte-offset cursor stored in a companion .offset file, enabling incremental reads without re-parsing the whole file. Handles file truncation (cursor beyond EOF) by resetting to 0. Only advances the cursor to the last complete newline to prevent partial-line reads and livelock.
relevance: claude-multi-agent-sdk worker polling uses a similar read loop; adopting offset cursors would eliminate redundant JSONL re-parsing and fix potential livelock when a line arrives mid-buffer

description: Prevents unbounded file growth by rotating inbox (by byte size) and outbox (by line count) when they exceed configurable thresholds. Rotation keeps the most recent half of messages, writes to a pid+timestamp temp file, then atomically renames to prevent partial reads. Resets the offset cursor after inbox rotation so the new file is read from byte 0.
code_snippet:
const kept = keepCount === 0 ? [] : lines.slice(-keepCount); // keep most recent half
const tmpPath = filePath + ".tmp." + process.pid + "." + Date.now();
writeFileWithMode(tmpPath, kept.join("
") + "
");
renameSync(tmpPath, filePath); // atomic rename
atomicWriteJson(cursorFile, { bytesRead: 0 }); // reset cursor after rotation
relevance: The researchers repo dispatch queues and spider outbox logs can apply this half-retention rotation to cap disk usage during long crawl runs with many agents

description: Graceful shutdown and draining are signaled via dedicated small signal files (worker.shutdown, worker.drain) rather than inline queue messages. Workers poll for these files each cycle. The lead writes them; the worker reads, acknowledges via outbox message, then deletes the file. Separating control signals from data messages prevents head-of-line blocking.
relevance: claude-multi-agent-dispatch can model graceful agent teardown with signal files instead of injecting control messages into the task queue, avoiding priority inversion during shutdown

description: Signals are extracted in three orthogonal layers: lexical (regex, no model calls), structural (parsing-based), and context (session state), then combined into a single ComplexitySignals object. This layered approach keeps fast heuristics separate from expensive parsing and allows each layer to be unit-tested independently.
code_snippet: export function extractAllSignals(prompt: string, context: RoutingContext): ComplexitySignals { return { lexical: extractLexicalSignals(prompt), structural: extractStructuralSignals(prompt), context: extractContextSignals(context) }; }
relevance: The researchers repo bloom-filter pre-routing can map to this: the bloom filter acts as a first-pass lexical gate (equivalent to extractLexicalSignals), allowing the DSPy dispatcher to focus only on structural+context signals for tasks that pass the bloom gate.

description: Complexity score is computed as the sum of weighted boolean/categorical signals from all three layers. Crucially, simple-task keywords carry a NEGATIVE weight (-2), allowing obvious low-complexity signals to cancel out spurious high-complexity ones. Tier thresholds are fixed integers (LOW<4, MEDIUM 4-7, HIGH>=8). A separate calculateConfidence() function converts distance-from-threshold into a 0.5-0.9 confidence value.
code_snippet: const WEIGHTS = { lexical: { architectureKeywords: 3, debuggingKeywords: 2, simpleKeywords: -2, riskKeywords: 2, questionDepthWhy: 2 }, structural: { subtasksMany: 3, crossFile: 2, impactSystemWide: 3, reversibilityDifficult: 2 }, context: { previousFailure: 2, previousFailureMax: 4 } }; const TIER_THRESHOLDS = { HIGH: 8, MEDIUM: 4 };
relevance: DSPy-based dispatch in the researchers repo currently uses model-generated scores. This additive weighted scheme can serve as a cheap pre-score that gates whether DSPy inference is even needed, running DSPy only when the additive score lands in the ambiguous 3-9 band.

description: Routing rules are plain objects with { name, condition(ctx, signals) => bool, action: { tier, reason }, priority: number }. Rules are sorted descending by priority and evaluated in order; the first match wins. Agent-specific rules (e.g. architect-complex-debugging at priority 85) override generic task-level rules (priority 55-70). A catch-all default-medium rule at priority 0 guarantees a result. Custom rules can override defaults by name via mergeRules().
code_snippet: { name: 'architect-complex-debugging', condition: (ctx, signals) => ctx.agentType === 'architect' && (signals.lexical.hasDebuggingKeywords || signals.lexical.hasArchitectureKeywords), action: { tier: 'HIGH', reason: 'Architect: Complex debugging/architecture decision' }, priority: 85 }
relevance: The researchers repo dispatch layer can adopt this DSL to encode spider-type overrides (e.g. bloom-miss-fallback rule at high priority) and crawl-complexity rules, allowing configuration without touching Python dispatch code.

description: After both the rules engine and the additive scorer independently produce a tier, the router compares them. If they diverge by more than 1 level, confidence is clamped to <=0.5 AND the higher tier wins (prefer over-provisioning over under-provisioning). This divergence guard prevents a rules miss or scorer miss from silently routing to the wrong model.
code_snippet: const divergence = Math.abs(ruleIdx - scoreIdx); if (divergence > 1) { confidence = Math.min(confidence, 0.5); finalTier = tierOrder[Math.max(ruleIdx, scoreIdx)]; reasons.push('Scorer/rules divergence: confidence reduced, preferred higher tier'); }
relevance: Researchers repo multi-agent dispatch can use the same pattern: when the bloom-filter pre-score and DSPy-inferred score disagree beyond a threshold, fall back to the higher-capability model and log the divergence for offline training data collection.

description: Each tier has a distinct prefix+suffix that sets a different cognitive contract. Haiku: strip filler words, 'TASK:' prefix, 'Return results directly. No preamble.' suffix. Sonnet: '## Task Execution Mode' prefix, 'Be thorough but efficient' suffix. Opus: '<thinking_mode>deep</thinking_mode>' prefix with multi-perspective reasoning guidelines, plus a 'Before Concluding' checklist suffix that asks the model to validate assumptions and check second-order effects.
code_snippet: // Haiku: HAIKU_PROMPT_PREFIX='TASK: '; suffix='Return results directly. No preamble.' // Sonnet: prefix='## Task Execution Mode\nExecute this task efficiently' // Opus: prefix='<thinking_mode>deep</thinking_mode>\nYou are operating at the highest capability tier...'
relevance: The researchers repo spider agents (crawl, extract, summarize) can use this pattern: Haiku spiders get stripped prompts for URL-list extraction; Sonnet spiders get structured output templates; Opus-tier analysis agents get the full reasoning-mode prefix with assumption-validation suffix.

description: The lexical signal extractor ranks question words by implied reasoning depth: 'why' > 'how' > 'what' > 'where' > none. This single ordinal signal contributes +2 points for 'why' queries and +1 for 'how' queries in the scorer, pushing root-cause and mechanism questions toward Opus while 'where is X' stays at Haiku. The regex matches both interrogative and imperative forms.
code_snippet: function detectQuestionDepth(prompt): 'why'|'how'|'what'|'where'|'none' { if (/\bwhy\b.*\?|\bwhy\s+(is|are|does|do)/i.test(prompt)) return 'why'; if (/\bhow\b.*\?|\bhow\s+(do|does|can)/i.test(prompt)) return 'how'; if (/\bwhat\b.*\?/i.test(prompt)) return 'what'; if (/\bwhere\b.*\?/i.test(prompt)) return 'where'; return 'none'; }
relevance: Researchers repo crawl tasks often start with 'why does X fail to index' vs 'where is the sitemap'. This ordinal depth signal, combined with the bloom pre-filter, can distinguish deep-analysis crawl tasks (Opus/Sonnet) from simple URL-resolution tasks (Haiku) without any model call.

description: AGENT_CATEGORY_TIERS maps agent categories (exploration, utility -> LOW; specialist, orchestration -> MEDIUM; advisor, planner, reviewer -> HIGH) as default starting points. However ALL agents are marked adaptive: agent-specific rules in rules.ts can override the category default based on actual task signals. An 'explore' agent stays Haiku for simple lookups but can be promoted to Opus if the task has architecture keywords. quickTierForAgent() provides the static default for callers who don't want full analysis.
code_snippet: export const AGENT_CATEGORY_TIERS: Record<string, ComplexityTier> = { exploration: 'LOW', utility: 'LOW', specialist: 'MEDIUM', orchestration: 'MEDIUM', advisor: 'HIGH', planner: 'HIGH', reviewer: 'HIGH' };
relevance: The researchers repo has named agents (bloom-pre-filter, dspy-dispatcher, scrapy-spider, summarizer). Mapping each to a category tier registry with task-adaptive override rules would let new agents inherit sensible defaults while still allowing signal-based promotion, directly parallel to how DSPy module selection should work.

description: The codebase explicitly deprecates reactive escalation (routeWithEscalation is marked @deprecated) in favor of proactive routing decided at delegation time. The orchestrator (highest-tier model) runs full signal analysis BEFORE handing off a subtask, choosing the right model upfront. Escalation logic (escalateModel, canEscalate) is retained only as a safety valve for unexpected failures, not as the primary dispatch path.
code_snippet: /** @deprecated Use getRoutingRecommendation for proactive routing. The orchestrator should analyze complexity upfront, not escalate reactively. */ export function routeWithEscalation(context, config) { return routeTask(context, config); }
relevance: The researchers repo currently triggers model upgrades only on failure. This pattern argues for inverting that: have the agenttasks orchestrator run signal analysis on each crawl job BEFORE dispatching, selecting spider model upfront. This eliminates wasted Haiku attempts on complex multi-domain crawls.

description: Two structural signals, reversibility ('easy'/'moderate'/'difficult') and impactScope ('local'/'module'/'system-wide'), are extracted via regex pattern lists and contribute directly to the score (reversibilityDifficult +2, impactSystemWide +3). Together they act as a risk dimension separate from complexity: a short task that touches production data still scores HIGH because reversibility='difficult' combined with riskKeywords pushes it over threshold 8.
code_snippet: function assessReversibility(prompt): 'easy'|'moderate'|'difficult' { const difficult = [/\bmigrat/i, /\bproduction\b/i, /\bdata.*loss/i, /\bdrop.*table/i, /\birreversible/i]; const moderate = [/\brefactor/i, /\brestructure/i, /\bchange.*schema/i]; if (difficult.some(p => p.test(prompt))) return 'difficult'; if (moderate.some(p => p.test(prompt))) return 'moderate'; return 'easy'; }
relevance: Researchers repo crawl jobs have a clear risk dimension: crawling a public docs site is low-risk; crawling and writing to a production index is high-risk. Encoding reversibility as a signal (e.g. 'update index', 'delete documents', 'reindex') in the bloom pre-filter vocabulary would let the routing layer catch risky writes without DSPy inference.

description: Claude Code shell hook scripts invoke a Node.js bridge (processHook) for complex logic. The bridge accepts a HookInput (event name + payload) and returns HookOutput (JSON response passed back to Claude Code). This decouples fast shell dispatch from rich TypeScript processing.
code_snippet: export { processHook, type HookInput, type HookOutput } from './bridge.js';
relevance: The researchers repo already uses shell hooks in .claude/settings.json; adopting a bridge pattern would let complex hook logic move into typed TypeScript modules while keeping the shell scripts as thin dispatchers.

description: The autopilot pipeline defines an ordered list of stage IDs (STAGE_ORDER: ralplan -> execution -> ralph -> qa). Each stage is implemented by a PipelineStageAdapter with shouldSkip(config), completionSignal, getPrompt(ctx), onEnter(ctx), and onExit(ctx). Stages can be skipped per config without changing pipeline logic. advanceStage() marks current as complete, calls onExit, finds next non-skipped, calls onEnter, and persists tracking atomically.
code_snippet: stages[currentStageIndex].status = 'complete'; stages[currentStageIndex].completedAt = new Date().toISOString(); currentAdapter?.onExit(context); tracking.currentStageIndex = nextIndex; stages[nextIndex].status = 'active'; nextAdapter.onEnter(context); writePipelineTracking(directory, tracking, sessionId); return { adapter: nextAdapter, phase: stages[nextIndex].id };
relevance: The researchers multi-agent dispatch repo coordinates agents across phases; this adapter pattern would let each agent role (crawler, summarizer, dispatcher) be a swappable pipeline stage with lifecycle hooks and completion signals.

description: AutopilotPhase is a discriminated union of named phases (expansion | planning | execution | qa | validation | complete | failed). Each phase has a dedicated state sub-object tracking its own completion flags. Phase transitions are triggered by explicit typed signals (AutopilotSignal: EXPANSION_COMPLETE, PLANNING_COMPLETE, etc.) detected in agent output, not by direct function calls.
code_snippet: export type AutopilotPhase = 'expansion' | 'planning' | 'execution' | 'qa' | 'validation' | 'complete' | 'failed'; export type AutopilotSignal = 'EXPANSION_COMPLETE' | 'PLANNING_COMPLETE' | 'EXECUTION_COMPLETE' | 'QA_COMPLETE' | 'VALIDATION_COMPLETE' | 'AUTOPILOT_COMPLETE';
relevance: The researchers dispatch layer needs robust phase tracking; modeling it as a typed state machine with signal-driven transitions prevents invalid state combinations and makes phase handoffs auditable in todos.jsonl.

description: detectExtractableMoment() scans assistant messages against typed pattern groups (problem-solution, technique, workaround, optimization, best-practice). Each group has a base confidence score (60-80). After matching, domain keyword co-occurrences add up to +15 boost. Returns DetectionResult with patternType, confidence 0-100, suggestedTriggers (max 5), and reason. shouldPromptExtraction() gates extraction behind a configurable threshold (default 60).
code_snippet: const triggerBoost = Math.min(suggestedTriggers.length * 5, 15); const finalConfidence = Math.min(bestMatch.confidence + triggerBoost, 100); return { detected: true, confidence: finalConfidence, patternType: bestMatch.type, suggestedTriggers: suggestedTriggers.slice(0, 5), reason: bestMatch.reason };
relevance: The researchers learner subsystem could apply confidence-scoring to detect when a crawl iteration has produced a reusable extraction pattern worth persisting to the skills store or todos.jsonl.

description: processMessageForSkills() maintains a Map<sessionId, Set<contentHash>> cache bounded to MAX_SESSIONS (100) using first-key LRU eviction. For each message it finds matching skills not already injected this session, marks them by contentHash, then registers formatted content with a shared contextCollector at priority=normal. Prevents duplicate injection within a session while staying memory-safe across concurrent sessions.
code_snippet: if (sessionCaches.size >= MAX_SESSIONS) { const firstKey = sessionCaches.keys().next().value; if (firstKey !== undefined) sessionCaches.delete(firstKey); } const newSkills = matchingSkills.filter(s => !injectedHashes.has(s.contentHash)); contextCollector.register(sessionId, { id: 'learner', source: 'learner', content, priority: 'normal' });
relevance: The agenttasks webapp manages multiple concurrent crawler sessions; this bounded-cache injection pattern could deduplicate context blocks injected into repeated agent prompts without unbounded memory growth.

description: saveProjectMemory() writes via atomicWriteJson() to prevent corruption on crash. withProjectMemoryLock() wraps any read-modify-write in an exclusive file lock (lockPathFor + withFileLock, 5000ms timeout). shouldRescan() compares lastScanned against CACHE_EXPIRY_MS so stale memory triggers a fresh detectProjectEnvironment() call.
code_snippet: export async function withProjectMemoryLock<T>(projectRoot: string, fn: () => T | Promise<T>): Promise<T> { return withFileLock(lockPathFor(getMemoryPath(projectRoot)), fn, { timeoutMs: 5000 }); } export function shouldRescan(memory: ProjectMemory): boolean { return Date.now() - memory.lastScanned > CACHE_EXPIRY_MS; }
relevance: The researchers claude-multi-agent-sdk has multiple agents writing shared state concurrently; adopting atomic writes + file locks with TTL-based rescan prevents corrupt-state bugs during parallel dispatch runs.

description: handleRecovery() attempts recovery in strict priority order: (1) context window limit - most critical; (2) session recovery - structural errors; (3) edit errors - tool-output level. Each layer returns { attempted, success }; cascade stops at first success. createRecoveryHook() exposes onError, afterToolExecute, isRecoverable, getRecoveryType lifecycle methods mapping to Claude Code hook events.
code_snippet: if (error) { const ctx = handleContextWindowRecovery(sessionId, error, config); if (ctx.attempted && ctx.success) return ctx; } if (error) { const ses = await handleSessionRecovery(sessionId, error, message, config); if (ses.attempted && ses.success) return ses; } if (toolName && toolOutput) { const edit = handleEditErrorRecovery(toolName, toolOutput); if (edit.attempted && edit.success) return edit; } return { attempted: false, success: false };
relevance: The researchers dispatch layer needs error recovery for long-running crawl jobs; this cascade pattern ensures most-impactful failures (context exhaustion) are caught first, avoiding wasted retries on lower-priority errors.

description: writeTrackingState() batches concurrent writes via a per-directory debounce map (100ms). On flush, mergeTrackerStates() reconciles disk state with in-memory pending state: counters use Math.max to avoid double-counting, agents merge by agent_id with newer-timestamp-wins semantics, last_updated takes max. A flushInProgress Set guards duplicate concurrent flushes. Stale agent detection uses 5-min threshold plus isProcessAlive() OS liveness checks.
code_snippet: const total_spawned = Math.max(diskState.total_spawned, pendingState.total_spawned); for (const agent of pendingState.agents) { const existing = agentMap.get(agent.agent_id); if (!existing) { agentMap.set(agent.agent_id, agent); } else { if (pendingTime >= existingTime) agentMap.set(agent.agent_id, agent); } }
relevance: The researchers claude-multi-agent-dispatch spawns many concurrent subagents; this debounced merge prevents write storms and state corruption when multiple agents complete near-simultaneously, a known pain point in the 252 dispatch tests.

description: flow-tracer.ts exposes narrow single-purpose functions (recordHookFire, recordHookResult, recordKeywordDetected, recordSkillActivated, recordSkillInvoked, recordModeChange) that each call appendReplayEvent() with a typed payload. All functions are best-effort (never throw). This creates an immutable session-scoped event log that reconstructs the full orchestration trace for post-mortem debugging without coupling tracing into individual hook implementations.
code_snippet: export function recordHookResult(directory, sessionId, hookName, hookEvent, durationMs, contextInjected, contextLength) { appendReplayEvent(directory, sessionId, { agent: 'system', event: 'hook_result', hook: hookName, hook_event: hookEvent, duration_ms: durationMs, context_injected: contextInjected, context_length: contextLength }); }
relevance: The researchers ARCHITECTURE.md documents the full orchestration trace manually; adopting an append-only replay event stream in the dispatch layer would make that trace machine-readable, replacing manual docs with a live artifact.

description: registerProjectMemoryContext() calls shouldRescan() and, if stale, runs detectProjectEnvironment() for a fresh scan. Before overwriting, it copies customNotes, userDirectives, and hotPaths from the existing memory object into the new one. This preserves user-authored data and hot-path statistics across automatic rescans while keeping tech stack, build info, and language detection current.
code_snippet: if (!memory || shouldRescan(memory)) { const existing = memory; memory = await detectProjectEnvironment(projectRoot); if (existing) { memory.customNotes = existing.customNotes; memory.userDirectives = existing.userDirectives; memory.hotPaths = existing.hotPaths; } await saveProjectMemory(projectRoot, memory); }
relevance: The researchers agenttasks project context (todos.jsonl, ARCHITECTURE.md) is user-authored and should survive automated environment rescans; this preserve-user-data pattern is directly applicable when the session-start hook reinitialises project state.
